... Consequently, it equally takes a transformative theme within the narrative. 
The theme of transformable nature assumes the position of a narrative device reflecting the 
Poliphilo’s interior development through the beautification of the natural world of topography, 
and the refined beauty of the topiary of the gardens; that is, the position of a transformable theme 
of metamorphosis of garden and topography, and their corresponding symbolic content with the 
soul’s (anima’s) stage of transformation.  
The description of lovesickness in the narrative, mostly existing in the second book,...

<!-- Page 107 -->

 
duality allows Poliphilo’s narrative to continue through the transformation of intellect, also echoed 
in the topiary, metaphorically transforming the crude into the perfect.40 
As a further example, Poliphilo narrates the experience of the interior design before the left-
hand wing of the open-aired courtyard, being divided by 7 rectangles and a wreath depicting 7 
triumphs, ruled by 7 planets, likely...

(We traversed the holy island through the paths and ways marked out by the plantings of 
the fruitful gardens, which were green with perennial spring foliage … Sometimes we 
passed through the pleasant shade of arbours paved with flowering periwinkle under a roof 
of topiary work … and so we arrived gaily at a limpid and sacred spring that bubbled forth 
plentifully.) (HP, 369-71)

Through the influence of the court and the circulation of the 
first French editions, HP gradually became a point of reference for 
architects, garden and topiary art designers, writers, and others 
working in various crafts and disciplines. One could also argue for 
influences in several French emblem books on the level of themes. 
For instance, Pierre Coustau’s La pegme of 1560 contained two such 
examples; l’Empire de leurs femmes, which reminds...

haire . . . crisping and inanulating by their
eares,' Windolet (21), ' loopes or windolets
in diverse places,' and even of perimeter
(53) and topiary (116), words in quite a
different class.
The book also contains a number of
defunct architectural terms, which apparently
never entered into usage, and whose meaning
it is now rather difficult to apprehend: these
words are not recorded in O.E.D., and

... ushers him into the second room 
of "discourses and reason marueilously wouen"; Mnemosyna (Mem- 
ory) brings him to the Queen herself ([D 49r])."8 Poliphilus describes the 
extraordinary inner court with its "Chesse-boord" pavement (D 49v), 
iconographic wall schemes, grotesque decorations, and topiary (D 5or- 
51r). The "imperiall Maiestie" and richly bejeweled attire of the Queen 
(D 51v-52r) align her with late-sixteenth-century English royal portrai- 
ture, and Dallington reveals his own aesthetic competence by sliding in 
amongst the list of her chambers a gloss on "pinacloth"...

... to Venus. On the other hand, 
the illustrations to the corresponding text in Martin's 
edition provide us with a different interpretation. They 
show two of the seven-step structures as enclosures, in 
which three steps were used for climbing up, the fourth 
supported a decorated colonnade of topiary work, whilst 
the remaining three in the back -
so we are left to 
imagine -
were used to walk down to the original level. 20 
Thus the French illustrator omitted the terracing of the 
more refined gardens, which is not surprising, considering 
the lesser degree of terracing in the French...

... 
set of gardens on the subsequent terrace, and were laid 
out in the same alternated fashion. They both had a 
knotted border, which contained an inscription and the 
emblematic representation of an eagle and an eagle facing 
a pheasant, made out of plants. These designs were a 
combination oflow topiary work and knot design (figure 9). 
To understand the problematic aspects regarding knot 
gardens, it is necessary to say that it is not known with 
certainty whether these designs were ever really laid out 
and if they were, in what formY Not only are the innodature

reflect the cultural shift between medieval and humanist 
cultures. The molding of nature, such as the trimming of 
boxwood into topiary along paths (noted in the HP, and 
possibly taken from Alberti, or Pliny the elder) in com-
partmentalized, geometric garden sections, and the 
planting of botanical specimens in different sections to 
excite the senses, displays both the interest in physical 
stimulation not particularly...

curiosities, is played out not only with a firm sense of the intricate rules 
that govern it, but with a proper regard for the likely ambivalence of the 
outcome. Thus Cythera's fruit trees bear fruit whatever the season; yet 
the topiary is clipped every day. The natural world is as honoured as is 
the artistry of its reformation or reformulation. It is the false impositions 
of art that are excluded, what the English version called 'counterfeiting'38 
in the silk garden, with the full rhetorical weight of making false...
